_meta.about: Curated, human-verified resources per topic code. Keyed by code (e.g. MIND.01). Loaded at runtime by index.html and merged over the search fallback; any code without an entry keeps its Watch/Read search links. See CURATING.md for the schema and quality rubric, and run `npm run verify-links` after every change.
_meta.knownVerifierWarnings: britannica.com, newsinhealth.nih.gov, and aeon.co bot-wall or rate-limit automated checkers, so verify-links.js reports them as WARN. All three were confirmed to return HTTP 200 in a real browser on 2026-07-16 — WARN here means 'checker was blocked', not 'link is dead'.

MIND.01.note: Article explains the effect and cites the source study; deeper is the original Norton/Mochon/Ariely paper. No video cleared the bar (no dedicated, rigorous explainer), so Watch stays on search.

deeper.title: The IKEA effect: When labor leads to love (Norton, Mochon & Ariely, 2012)

MIND.02.note: Jarrett is the definitive 'the pop story is overhyped' piece; deeper is the Kilner & Lemon review (open access) that the hype critique rests on. Video left on search to avoid Ramachandran-style overclaiming.

article.title: Mirror Neurons: The Most Hyped Concept in Neuroscience?

deeper.title: What We Know Currently about Mirror Neurons (Kilner & Lemon, Current Biology 2013)

MIND.03.note: Schwartz's own TED talk is the claim; Collins + the Scheibehenne meta-analysis are the replication reality (the jam-study effect is near zero on average). Pairing them is the honest version of this topic.

deeper.title: Can There Ever Be Too Many Options? A Meta-Analytic Review of Choice Overload (Scheibehenne et al., 2010)

MIND.04.note: The hook already frames synchronicity as contested and 'pattern hunger'. Rather than a credulous Jung explainer, the curated Read is Shermer on patternicity — the skeptical mechanism critics point to. Left video on search.

article.title: Patternicity: Finding Meaningful Patterns in Meaningless Noise

MIND.05.note: Neuroplasticity is heavily hyped; Britannica is a sober, non-salesy overview (avoids the 'rewire your brain' self-help genre). No video cleared the rigor bar, so Watch stays on search.

MIND.06.note: Britannica for the concept; deeper is the founding Festinger & Carlsmith (1959) forced-compliance experiment (open PDF, MIT course page).

deeper.title: Cognitive consequences of forced compliance (Festinger & Carlsmith, 1959)

MIND.07.note: The catalog frames the statistical critique as the interesting half, so the curated Read is Jarry's 'probably not real' — the effect is largely reproduced by random data (autocorrelation). This is the whole point of the entry.

article.title: The Dunning-Kruger Effect Is Probably Not Real
article.source: Jonathan Jarry, McGill Office for Science and Society

MIND.08.note: Csikszentmihalyi's own TED talk (primary source) plus a Society for Neuroscience piece on the neuroscience of flow, rather than a productivity-guru take.

article.title: Turning on Flow Means Turning Off Parts of the Brain

MIND.09.note: TED-Ed for the accessible mechanism; Harvard's Healthy Sleep for the overview; deeper is the Rasch & Born review (open access), the standard citation. Deliberately not leaning on Walker's contested popular claims.

video.title: The benefits of a good night's sleep

article.title: Sleep On It: How Snoozing Strengthens Memories

deeper.title: About Sleep's Role in Memory (Rasch & Born, Physiological Reviews 2013)

MIND.10.note: Anil Seth's TED talk and Aeon essay are the most accessible on-ramp to 'the brain predicts, not receives'; deeper is Andy Clark's field-defining 'Whatever next?' (open PDF).

video.title: Your brain hallucinates your conscious reality

article.title: The hard problem of consciousness is a distraction from the real one

deeper.title: Whatever next? Predictive brains, situated agents, and the future of cognitive science (Clark, 2013)

MIND.11.note: Nielsen's essay is the best single piece on spaced repetition as a practice, not just a definition — the forgetting curve, why self-made cards matter, and how it builds understanding.

MIND.12.note: Jarry's piece both debunks the '38 witnesses' fabrication and reports the modern CCTV evidence (help is common, and more likely with more bystanders) — exactly the correction the topic's hook points at.

article.title: The Bystander Effect Started from a Lie
article.source: Jonathan Jarry, McGill Office for Science and Society
